stimulus: Researchers Estela Cruz and Maya Stenberg analyzed survey data tracking U.S. citizens' political engagement (on a 7-point scale) and their level of civic knowledge in a recent national midterm election. The team claims that, regardless of political engagement, voters with higher civic knowledge are consistently more likely to actually cast a ballot than voters with lower civic knowledge.
stem: Which choice best describes data in the table that support Cruz and Stenberg's claim?

answerOptions.content: At each point on the political engagement scale, high-civic-knowledge voters were more likely to vote than low-civic-knowledge voters.
Only low-civic-knowledge voters who scored a 2 on the engagement scale had a voting probability below 40%.
The closer that low-civic-knowledge voters are to the ends of the engagement scale, the more likely they were to vote.
High-civic-knowledge voters were more likely to score at the extreme ends of the engagement scale than low-civic-knowledge voters were.

rationale:
Choice A is the best answer because it describes data in the table that directly support Cruz and Stenberg's claim. At every engagement-scale value from 1 through 7, the high-civic-knowledge column shows a voting probability higher than the low-civic-knowledge column (62% vs. 42%, 58% vs. 38%, and so on through 91% vs. 69%). That across-the-board advantage is exactly what the claim asserts.

Choice B is incorrect because it is factually wrong: low-civic-knowledge voters at engagement-scale value 2 have a voting probability of 38% (below 40%), but the claim that they are the “only” such group is not what the researchers' claim asks about—the claim concerns the across-knowledge comparison, not isolated low values. Choice C is incorrect because, although the table shows that low-civic-knowledge voters at higher engagement values vote more, the closer-to-the-ends framing does not isolate the comparison between knowledge groups that the claim is about. Choice D is incorrect because the table does not show how voters' engagement scores are distributed by civic-knowledge level—only the voting probability within each cell.
